reply:
The statement by Jesus, "the Father is greater than I," is found in John 14:28. Understanding this statement requires a look at Christian theological concepts, especially the doctrine of the Trinity.

In mainstream Christian theology, the Trinity describes God as one God in three persons: the Father, the Son (Jesus Christ), and the Holy Spirit. These three persons are distinct yet co-equal and co-eternal. They share the same divine essence but have different roles or relational distinctions within the Godhead.

When Jesus says, "the Father is greater than I," it is typically understood in the context of Jesus' incarnation — that is, Jesus taking on human nature. In his earthly ministry, Jesus chose to take on a subordinate role, willingly submitting to the Father's will in the plan of salvation. This statement reflects the functional hierarchy in the economy of salvation, not inequality in divinity or nature. 

Theologians often explain that Jesus is speaking from the perspective of his human experience; he is emphasizing his humility and obedience. Within the same framework, the Holy Spirit is not considered lesser in essence or divinity, but the Spirit's role is distinct, functioning as the helper or advocate.

So, within orthodox Trinitarian theology, Jesus' statement is not seen as indicating a hierarchy of divinity, but a relational and functional distinction. All three persons of the Trinity are fully and equally God, sharing the same divine nature.
